meta.subject: physics
meta.topic_name: Graph Between Applied Force and Friction

intro.kicker: Topic 5 of 38 • Chapter: Friction • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.topicName: Graph Between Applied Force and Friction
intro.description: The friction vs applied force graph (Fig. 5.2 in NCERT) shows three distinct regions: OA (static friction, self-adjusting, rises linearly with applied force), point A (limiting friction, peak), and BC (kinetic friction, constant and less than limiting). The key visual message is that kinetic friction is less than maximum static friction and is independent of applied force. NEET directly presents this graph and asks analytical questions about each region.

expectedQuestions.description: 1 graph-based MCQ per paper. NEET presents the friction vs applied force graph and asks: region identification, which segment is parallel to x-axis, the significance of point A, or which represents the drop from static to kinetic.

timeRequired.description: 45 min: 15 min graph analysis, 30 min MCQ on graph segment identification. Sketch the graph from memory and label all segments. The most common error is mislabelling which region represents limiting vs kinetic friction.

difficulty.description: Visual recall topic. Once the graph shape is memorized, answering takes 30 seconds. The trap: segment BC is parallel to the x-axis (kinetic friction is constant and does NOT change with applied force). Students who confuse BC as an inclined region will select the wrong answer.

nriUsaGap.description: AP Physics and NEET both include the static-kinetic transition graph. NEET is more likely to present the graph with numerical axis labels and ask for the value at point A or gradient of OA. Ensure you can read off numerical values from a given friction-applied force graph.

bulletNotes: Direct graph identification questions appear in most NEET papers.
Region BC (kinetic, parallel to x-axis) is the single most tested segment.
Point A (limiting friction peak) appears in ‘which point represents limiting friction’ questions.

strategySteps.title: Memorise the Four Segments
strategySteps.description: OA: static friction (Fs = applied force, linear rise). Point A: limiting friction (peak, Fs = Fl = μs R). A to B: brief drop as motion begins (transition from static to kinetic). BC: kinetic friction (constant, Fk = μk R, parallel to x-axis).

strategySteps.title: Emphasise BC is Horizontal
strategySteps.description: Kinetic friction does NOT change with applied force — segment BC is parallel to the x-axis. This is the most tested feature. Contrast with OA which has slope = 1 (friction equals applied force in the linear static region).

strategySteps.title: Link Graph to Coefficient Inequalities
strategySteps.description: The graph visually confirms Fk < Fl (μk < μs). The drop from A (peak) to BC (plateau) is the graphical representation of this inequality. NEET MCQs connect the graph to the coefficient relationship.

cards.title: Friction Graph Complete Notes
cards.description: Graph analysis with labelled segments, coefficient relationships, physical significance of each segment, and 8 solved NEET graph MCQs.

cards.title: Friction Graph Quick Reference
cards.description: One-page printable graph with all 4 regions labelled (OA, A, A-B, BC), formulas at each point, and segment properties table.

subtopicsIndex.sectionTitle: Subtopics in Graph Between Applied Force and Friction

cards.title: Four Regions of the Friction Graph
cards.coreRevision: OA: static friction = applied force (slope = 1, linear). Point A: limiting friction Fl = μs R (peak value). A-to-B: friction drops briefly as motion begins. BC: kinetic friction Fk = μk R (constant, parallel to x-axis). Fk < Fl always.
points: Region OA: Fs equals applied force P; object at rest; self-adjusting
Point A: maximum static friction, Fl = μs R; motion about to begin
A → B: slight drop as static friction hand-off to kinetic; irregularities unlock
Region BC: Fk = constant = μk R; parallel to x-axis regardless of applied force
Gradient of OA = 1 (friction tracks applied force exactly in static regime)
cards.example: If μs = 0.5 and the block weighs 100 N, then A is at F = 50 N. Once the block starts moving (μk = 0.4), kinetic friction = 40 N along BC, regardless of whether applied force is 50 N or 200 N.

cards.title: Key Features to Identify on the Graph
cards.coreRevision: NEET questions present the graph and ask: what does segment OA represent? What is the physical significance of point A? Why is segment BC parallel to x-axis? What does the drop A→B represent?
points: OA represents static friction (self-adjusting, not yet limiting)
A = limiting friction (peak, first onset of motion)
A→B drop = friction decreases as motion starts (kinetic < static)
BC = kinetic friction (constant, motion-dependent but speed-independent)
BC parallel to x-axis = kinetic friction does NOT depend on applied force
cards.example: MCQ: ‘In the graph, segment BC is parallel to the applied force axis. This shows that kinetic friction is —’ Answer: independent of applied force (constant once motion starts).

curriculum.hintText: AP Physics vs NEET on the friction vs applied force graph.

gaps.title: Segment A→B Transition
gaps.description: AP Physics introduces the static-kinetic transition but does not emphasise the brief drop (A→B) between maximum static and kinetic friction as a testable graph feature. NEET includes this drop and asks why it occurs.
bullets: The A→B drop occurs because as motion starts, surface irregularities no longer have time to fully interlock.
NEET MCQ: ‘On the friction-applied force graph, segment AB shows a brief decrease. This indicates that —’ Answer: kinetic friction is less than limiting friction.

gaps.title: Graphical Proof of μk < μs
gaps.description: NEET uses the graph as a visual proof of the coefficient inequality. The y-coordinate at A > y-coordinate along BC visually confirms Fl > Fk, hence μs > μk. This graphical-to-algebraic connection is tested directly.
bullets: y at A = Fl = μs R; y along BC = Fk = μk R. Since A is higher than BC, μs > μk.
This graphical observation is a NEET-style ‘what can be concluded from this graph’ question.

questions.question: In the graph between force of friction and applied force, the segment BC (kinetic region) is parallel to the x-axis. This implies that kinetic friction is:

options.text: Independent of applied force and remains constant

questions.explanation: Segment BC parallel to the x-axis means the friction value (y-axis) does not change as applied force (x-axis) increases. Kinetic friction Fk = μk R is constant once the body is in motion, regardless of how large the applied force is.

questions.question: On the friction-applied force graph, point A represents:
questions.explanation: Point A is the peak of the graph: the friction force reaches its maximum value just before motion begins. This maximum value of static friction is called limiting friction (Fl = μs R).

questions.question: In the graph between applied force and friction, the linear region OA represents:
Why does friction decrease momentarily between point A and B in the friction-applied force graph?
options: Motion starts and kinetic friction (μk < μs) replaces static friction
questions.answer: Motion starts and kinetic friction (μk < μs) replaces static friction

questions.question: In the friction-applied force graph, what does the gradient of segment OA equal?
From the friction vs applied force graph, which information can be directly read?
options: Coefficient of static and kinetic friction
questions.answer: Coefficient of static and kinetic friction

items.title: Sketch the complete friction vs applied force graph
items.description: Origin O → linear rise OA (static) → peak at A (limiting) → slight drop A-B → horizontal plateau BC (kinetic). Sketch from memory with all labels.

items.title: Label all four regions correctly
items.description: OA: static (Fs = P); A: limiting (Fl = μs R); A-B: transition drop; BC: kinetic (Fk = μk R, constant). Misidentifying any segment costs a direct mark.

items.title: State why BC is parallel to x-axis
items.description: Kinetic friction is independent of applied force. Once the body is moving, Fk = μk R is fixed by normal reaction alone.

items.title: Connect graph to coefficient inequality
items.description: y-value at A > y-value along BC means Fl > Fk, proving μs > μk. This is the graphical proof of the coefficient inequality.

items.question: What is the physical meaning of the slope of segment OA being exactly 1?
items.answer: In the static regime (OA), static friction self-adjusts to exactly equal the applied force. So as applied force increases by 1 N, static friction increases by exactly 1 N — slope = 1. This continues until the applied force reaches Fl (point A). Beyond A, the body begins to move and the self-adjusting mechanism breaks down.

items.question: Why does friction drop from A to B when motion starts?
items.answer: In the static state, surface irregularities have fully interlocked over the contact time. When motion begins, these interlocked irregularities disengage and one surface skims over the other at speed — the real contact area (and bonding time) decreases. This creates a lower kinetic friction. The drop from A to B represents this decrease from μs R to μk R.

items.question: If applied force exceeds point A, can friction increase again?
items.answer: No. Once motion has started, friction stays at the kinetic value Fk = μk R (segment BC), which is constant. Applying more force increases the body’s acceleration but does NOT increase the friction force. Fk depends only on μk and R, not on the applied force.

items.question: Is the friction-applied force graph the same for all surfaces?
items.answer: The shape is qualitatively the same (OA rising, peak at A, drop to BC plateau), but the y-coordinates depend on the specific μs and μk values of the surface pair. Smoother surfaces have lower A-peaks; rougher surfaces have higher peaks. The ratio Fl/Fk = μs/μk is typically in the range 1.1–1.5 for common materials.

grounding_summary: Page 237 describes the friction vs applied force graph with four explicit points: OA represents static friction rising linearly, A is maximum static (limiting) friction, BC represents kinetic friction (constant), and the BC segment is parallel to the x-axis.
The textbook explicitly states: ‘kinetic friction does not change with the applied force, it remains constant, whatever be the applied force’ — directly grounding the BC plateau feature.
The textbook links this graph to the inequality Fk < Fl, underpinning the visual proof of μk < μs.
grounding_excerpt: Part $OA$ of the curve represents static friction $(F_s)$. Its value increases linearly with the applied force
At point $A$ the static friction is maximum. This represents limiting friction $(F_l)$.
As the portion $BC$ of the curve is parallel to $x$-axis therefore kinetic friction does not change with the applied force, it remains constant, whatever be the applied force.

review.notes: Content grounded in page-237 OCR markdown. All four graph regions covered from NCERT text. Verbatim excerpts verified. No dual_cta.
